data.title: Selling Norfolk Southern Corporation
data.description:
It’s been a little over a year since I changed my stance on Norfolk Southern Corporation (NYSE:NSC) and turned bullish. Since then, the shares have returned about 38.3% against a gain of about 36.5% for the S&P 500, which is gratifying enough. The company has reported earnings since, obviously, so I want to review those to see if it makes sense to buy more, hold, or take profits on this stock and move the capital elsewhere. I’ll make this determination by looking at the health of the financial results, and the valuation, especially on a relative basis. Before getting into it, I want to spend some time sermonizing, so I’m going to sermonize. In my view, successful investing requires a willingness to go against the crowd. When the crowd is overly pessimistic about a company, you want to buy. At the same time, though, when the crowd gets too sanguine about a given company and drives a stock to lofty heights, you need to be willing to sell.

Those who read me regularly know that I offer a thesis statement near the beginning of each of my articles. This offers more than you might glean from the bullet points above, but less than you’d get from the entire article. The thesis statement allows you to understand my thinking up front so you can avoid the terrible humour and proper spelling that you might find in one of my typical articles. You’re welcome. I’ll be selling my Norfolk Southern shares. I think the valuation is now excessive, especially in light of the fact that the company is less profitable now than it was prior to the pandemic. The combination of decade high valuations and drastically lower profitability is never a good sign in my view, so I’m taking my winnings and leaving.

Contractual Obligations

Before getting into the heart of the analysis, I think it’s worthwhile pointing out what is in store for the company over the next few years. I think the technical term is “spicy meatball”, per the chart below. This year, the company is on the hook for about $3.25 billion, and an average of $1.7 billion in each of the next two years. The company currently has about $659 million in cash, so they’re going to need to tap capital or debt markets for some portion of these obligations. I don’t know if you’ve been keeping track, but interest rates are rather elevated at the moment, so there is risk that the company will be rolling debt at higher rates of interest. I think this provides an interesting backdrop for the remainder of the analysis.

Financial Snapshot

The first six months of the year haven’t been great in my estimation. Relative to the same period last year, revenue and net income are lower by 1% and 3.9% respectively. Additionally, the capital structure has deteriorated materially, with long term debt up by a whopping $2.193 billion, or 14.6%. In case you forgot about how bad 2023 was, I will remind you. In that year, net income was about 44% lower than it was in 2022. Thus, it’s not like the company is being benchmarked against a banner year. While on the topic of 2022, I would note that net income for the first six months of this year is lower than the same period in 2022 by $732 million, or 48%. If you’re itching to know how the most recent six-month period did relative to the same period prior to the pandemic, get ready to have that particular itch scratched. Net income for the first six months of 2024 was $609 million lower than it was the same period in 2019.

Given the above, I’m only willing to add to the position at the right price. If the valuation is too rich, I’m going to sell the stock.

The Stock

The phrase “at the right price” is very important in my view, because even a great, profitable company with a great “moat” can be a terrible investment if you overpay for it. Similarly, a company that is less profitable today than it was five years ago, such as this one, may be a great investment if you can pick it up at a decent price. The idea of buying cheap sounds great, but it’s a bit amorphous. I need to be a bit more concrete.

I apply a few metrics to decide if a stock is trading cheaply enough or not. Some of these are more complex than others. On the simple side, I look at the relationship between price and some measure of economic value like sales, or earnings, and the like. The lower the price per unit of economic value, the better. In particular, I want to see shares trading at a discount both to the overall market and to their own history. On this score, I think it's fair to say that the current valuation of Norfolk Southern is rather high. Specifically, the market is paying $4.79 for $1 of sales for this business that is less profitable now than it was in 2019. Per the following, when the market has reached these levels in the past, the shares have subsequently dropped in price. Additionally, on a PE basis, the stock is trading near a decade high. This is very troublesome in my view.

Source: YCharts

Source: YCharts

In addition to looking at simple ratios, I want to try to understand what the market is currently "thinking" about a given company's future. In order to do this, I turn to the work of Stephen Penman, particularly his book "Accounting for Value." One of the most interesting ideas expressed in this book is that share price itself is a valuable source of information, and gives us clues about what the market view of the future of the business is. So, if the market’s assumptions are too optimistic, that’s bad.

We get at the assumptions by applying some high school algebra to work out the “g” (growth) variable in a standard finance formula. The greater the expectations, the more risky the investment. According to this approach, the market currently "thinks" that Norfolk Southern will grow at a rate of about 6.6% from current levels. In my view, that is excessive for a company that is less profitable today than it was in 2019. This is yet another reason to avoid this stock at current levels.

data.title: Citigroup: I Am Buying Every Dip
data.description:
Citigroup (NYSE:C) delivered strong earnings in Q3-2024 which I will discuss later in this article. However, the stock fell 5% on the day partly due to profit-taking (the stock outperformed in the last 12 months) but mostly due to (unwarranted) concerns about fears of additional regulatory outreach (asset cap). I will also discuss why I believe the concern of further regulatory intrusion is unlikely. Still, first I would like to set out the opportunity and how that should feed into the valuation of the stock.

Forward Valuation of Citi Stock

Citigroup is currently trading at ~0.7x Tangible Book Value (TBV). The current TBV is ~$90; by the end of 2025, it will likely be ~$100 and ~$110 by the end of 2026. Citi's management is committed to RoTCE of ~11% to 12% by 2026. If we assume Citi hits its numbers, the stock should be valued somewhere in the range of 1.1x to 1.5x TBV or somewhere in the range of $120 to $170.

The above valuation naturally depends on Citi meeting its RoTCE target and at the high end of the valuation range also reflects (i) expectation of further improvement in returns beyond 2026 (ii) recognition that the quality of the Citi business model is much improved with stable, capital-light businesses such as Wealth and Services becoming a bigger part of the firm.

There is also additional potential upside from outsized share buybacks as capital is released from the Mexico IPO, finalization of the Basel III end-game capital rules, and lower capital requirements for Citi attributed to its much-simplified business model.

Of course, the Street is skeptical that Citi will meet its targets. Analysts believe that Citi will come short on Revenue (4% to 5% CAGR) and Expenses as they may need to spend more on regulatory fixes.

Even if we assume that Citi falls short of the returns target and only delivers 10% RoTCE by 2026 then the market may only reward Citi with a 0.8x TBV valuation. The share price by 2026, should be approximately $88. So in short, at a share price of $64 and a safe dividend of ~3%, there is a sufficient margin of safety in the current share price and upside optionality.

The key downside risks are macroeconomic and/or geopolitical shocks. The mitigating factor is that Citi, like all of the large U.S. banks, has effectively been derisked by over 15 years of regulatory reform that resulted in lower balance sheet risks and much higher capital and liquidity buffers. The other risk factor is regulatory outreach and intrusion.

There Is No Asset Cap

Mike Mayo, incidentally having Citigroup as his top pick, asked a question during the earnings call that caused the share price to dip as well as caught the management team completely unprepared. The question was as per below:

Mike Mayo

And I guess I'd highlight, as you know, on October 2nd, Senator Warren asked the OCC to impose growth restrictions because Citi is “too big to manage”. Now I would assume you don't agree with that, but still the question that a lot of people have is, what assurances can you give that an asset cap won't happen at Citigroup? Thanks

It seems that Citi's CEO was completely unprepared for this question and instead of simply immediately responding "There is no asset cap and we do not expect one", she provided a long-winded answer about the transformation and that she is:

very confident about the strategy we've laid out for the firm, the deliberate path we're on, the huge progress we've already made, and that we will continue making with determination and with clarity going forward.

Mr. Market interpreted this as a real risk and that Mike Mayo must know something that the market is unaware of. At that point, the stock dipped 5% and did not recover for the day.

Only much later on the call, Jane Fraser addressed the point directly:

So let me be crystal clear. We do not have an asset cap and there are no additional measures other than what was announced in July in place and not expecting any.

The Regulatory Concerns Are Over-Blown

Some context is important here. For unknown reasons, Mike Mayo brought up the asset cap as a potential risk presumably as it was applied in the Wells Fargo misconduct and misselling issues.

This is a completely different matter to delays in one of the workstreams dealing with Citi's consent order. It is mostly a technology and process issue and not customer or misconduct-related. It is simply ensuring complete and accurate data integrity feeding into regulatory reports the Group produces. Whilst it is undoubtedly a serious matter, it is resolvable within a relatively short time and more importantly an asset cap remedy could not feasibly be on the cards for such an issue.

Strong Q3-2024 Performance

Citi delivered a strong set of results with positive operating leverage across all divisions.

  • Services revenue increased by 8% year on year fueled by fee income growth and a stellar performance from Security Services with 24% year-over-year revenue growth. Services delivered an exceptional 26.4% RoTCE for Q2-2024
  • Markets benefited from a strong performance from Prime Finance in equities (recurring income) somewhat offset by lower income in FICC (compared with a very strong prior year comparison). It still managed to deliver 1% revenue growth year on year whereas expenses decreased by 1% year on year.
  • Banking delivered a very strong performance with Investment Banking fees up 44% driven by DCM (+75%). This has been driven by capital markets recovery but also by Citi gaining market share.
  • Wealth delivered 9% revenue growth year on year whereas expenses declined by 4% as management right-sized its operations and delivered strong operating leverage.
  • US Personal Banking ("USPB") also delivered operating leverage including 3% revenue growth year on year and a 1% decrease in expenses.

The full-year 2024 guidance also confirmed that Citi is expected to meet both Revenue (expected at the higher end) and Expense targets.

Final Thoughts

Mr. Market is not giving Citi much credit despite clear progress and is highly skeptical of Citi's ability to deliver on the transformations and 2026 targets. I see 2025 as being the inflection year where doubters may just become believers. The upside is very significant coupled with a high margin of safety, I see the investment case as asymmetric.

The catalysts are multiple and include increased share buybacks, continued revenue growth, and progress on resolving the consent orders. At 0.7x TBV, I remain very bullish and continue to accumulate on dips.

data.title: TSMC Defies Market Concerns With Q3 Earnings Beat
data.description:
Investment Thesis

TSMC (NYSE:TSM) has rallied 13% since our last coverage, narrowing the gap toward our target price of $210. Following its Q3 2024 earnings, which showed substantial year-over-year (YoY) revenue and profit growth, we believe TSMC’s long-term prospects remain intact. The company’s rapid growth is a testament to its success in fulfilling tech giants’ expanding demands. Advanced technologies like 3-nm and impending 2-nm chips place TSMC in a better position to satisfy this growing demand.

Its strategic focus on AI and high-performance computing (HPC) keeps us bullish on the company growing fast and reaching higher margins than traditional segments. Despite the high CapEx being a concern, it’s necessary to equip TSMC as the leading supplier of advanced chips. Innovation and a dominant market share have been the pillars of TSMC’s success, ensuring steady future revenue and profitable growth.

TSMC Delivers Strong Q3 2024: Powered by AI and HPC Demand, Revenues Surge 39% YoY

TSMC had a strong Q3 of 2024, posting revenues of NT$759.69 billion, approximately $23.5 billion, up 39% YoY and 12.8% quarter-over-quarter (QoQ) compared to Q2/2024. The company reported Q3 GAAP EPS of $1.94, beating estimates by $0.15, with revenue of $23.5 billion exceeding expectations by $210 million. Net incomes during the same period were up 54.2% YoY at NT$325.26 billion, reflecting the company’s ability to capitalize on highly advanced chips in AI and HPC applications.

Not surprisingly, Q3 revenue has exceeded the analyst consensus forecast of ~NT$748 billion (~$23.21 billion), signaling that TSMC’s revenue generation capacity exceeds the street standards based on its AI-technology-related chips. Against Q2 2024 (NT$673.51 billion), the sequential quarterly growth of 12.8% marks the robustness of AI-driven demand in propelling the company’s top-line growth.

Moreover, the key driver for TSMC’s substantial profitability growth is a net income increase of 31.2% QoQ and an underlined operational efficiency to scale up production to meet increasing demand. Strategic focus on high-margin sectors like AI, HPC, and 5G continues to drive demand for advanced chips and help TSMC achieve more substantial margins and revenue growth.

Remarkably, September 2024’s YoY revenue increased by 39.6%, directly corresponding with the increasing demand for AI chips. Driven by the need for HPC, TSMC is considered a lead supplier of critical technology to tech giants like Apple (AAPL), Nvidia (NVDA), and AMD (AMD) under AI adoption and may continue to support its top-line growth.

Most importantly, though, the strong position of TSMC in leading-edge processes, the 3nm process in particular, is a very real and tangible bridging point between these revenues at the very top and ongoing advancements in tech. For Q2 2024, the 3nm technology accounted for 15% of wafer revenue from 6% in Q3 2023. This growth reflects the market’s increasing reliance on smaller, more efficient chips for AI, smartphones, and HPC applications. For instance, 59% of the company’s total Q3 2023 revenue stemmed from advanced technologies (7nm and below). Now, this share expanded to 67% by Q2 2024.

In Q3 2024, the 3nm, 5nm, and 7nm technologies together accounted for 69% of the total wafer revenue, compared to 67% in Q2 2024. This means that TSMC still leads the process race in semiconductor manufacturing, while expanding its share in the rapidly growing AI and HPC markets.

Besides, the move to value chains of advanced technologies underpins TSMC’s ability to maintain its robust margins, with the gross margin reaching 57.8% in Q3 2024. Continuing revenue growth and a steady cadence of adoptions at advanced nodes testify to the company’s leadership position in the semiconductor space and ability to meet future demand for a high-performance chip as markets evolve.

TSMC also claims that production of its 2nm process technology is targeted to begin in full-volume production in 2025. The N2 can provide a 10-15% speed improvement at the same power or a 25-30% power improvement at the same speed. With that, there is a 15% chip density increase over its 3nm predecessor.

The new tape-outs for 2nm in the first two years may outpace both the 3nm and 5nm technologies in their early production phases. This aggressive ramp-up of N2 technology mirrors the strong demand already seen in N3. Rapid adoption rates are fundamental to a secure pipeline of clients (like Nvidia and AMD) eager to adopt these energy-efficient solutions. However, these factors may not be integrated into the company’s consensus revenue growth estimates for 2025 and 2026.

Furthermore, the company’s extension of N2 technology with N2P provides an additional 5% performance or 5% to 10% power efficiency gains. N2P is slated for production in H2 2026. These simultaneous enhancements provide TSMC with a solid competitive advantage in performance and efficiency.

Moreover, the N2P technology supports both smartphone and HPC applications that may serve the dual demands of consumer electronics and enterprise computing. For instance, HPC contributed 42% of total revenues in Q3 2023. By Q2, 2024, this share had risen significantly to 52% as more industries increasingly require advanced computing capabilities (particularly in AI, data analytics, and machine learning).

In contrast, smartphone revenue, traditionally one of TSMC’s core segments, has declined from 39% in Q3 2023 to 33% in Q2 2024. While this decline may appear concerning, it points to a strategic pivot towards HPC and AI. Here, margins are typically higher, and long-term growth potential is greater. Hence, this strategic shift justifies the focus on developing 2nm and 3nm chips.

In other words, the global AI market will be over $638 billion in 2024 and may reach ~3.7 trillion by 2034 at a CAGR of 19.1% (2024-2034). On the other hand, revenues across the US AI market stood at 123.07 billion in 2023 and may surpass ~851 billion by 2034, growing at an annual rate of 19.3% (2024-2034).

Finally, TSMC’s top line protects against regional economic issues. For instance, North America remains TSMC’s largest market and consistently contributes a major portion of revenue. The region accounted for 69% of the revenue in Q3 2023 and 65% in Q2 2024. Similarly, China’s revenue share increased from 9% in Q1 2024 to 16% in Q2, reflecting the expanding demand for semiconductors in this region despite US-China tensions.

Massive CapEx and Cash Flow Strain

A structural weakness in TSMC’s growth potential derives from its massive CapEx that considerably strains its cash flow and financials. Considering the quarterly trends from Q3 2023 through Q3 2024, CapEx constantly exceeded NT$170 billion per quarter. In Q3 2023, CapEx was NT$226.62 billion, dropping to NT$170.16 billion in Q4 2023. Then, it rose to NT$181.3 billion, NT$205.68 billion, and NT$207.08 in Q1, Q2, and Q3 2024, respectively.

These are massive capital outlays to maintain TSMC’s competitive edge as it invests in advanced node manufacturing processes (3nm and 2nm). However, such heavy investments can hinder short-term profitability and pressure cash flow. This may hurt stock valuation based on turns in the street sentiment if returns on these investments do not materialize as quickly as anticipated.

For instance, TSMC has experienced a gradual erosion in its ROIC that signals inefficiencies in capital deployment and challenges in maintaining return trends as its scale and CapEx requirements grow. TSMC’s ROIC in 2014 was 21.83%, and for a couple of years, it ranged evenly around ~21%; then, it slowly drifted downward and hit 19.86% by 2018, continually falling to 18.78% in 2019.

Although the company saw a resumption in its ROIC in 2020 (reaching 25.19%), this upward trend was temporary. By 2023, TSMC’s ROIC had dropped to 19.81%; in 2024, TTM’s ROIC was flat at 19.74%. The decline in ROIC suggests that the company is generating less profit from its capital investments than in previous years. This is attributed to the increasing complexity and cost of advanced semiconductors that may not yield proportionally high returns. This eroding profitability from capital investment directly impacts the stock valuations, as ongoing projects may struggle to justify the enormous capital outlays based on current ROIC trends.

Takeaway

TSMC’s strong revenue momentum, driven by AI and high-performance computing, supports the corporation’s long-term growth prospects. Demand from major clients and new chip technology developments place TSMC in an excellent position to maintain its competitive advantage in this market. Despite high capital expenditures, its strategic focus on AI and HPC ensures continued revenue and profit expansion.

data.title: Starbucks: Big Unionization Problem
data.description:
Co-Authored By Noah Cox and Brock Heilig.

Investment Thesis

Starbucks (NASDAQ:SBUX) has been under a lot of pressure as the global coffee chain has been trying to buck a slowdown and bring in new management to set up a turnaround. The Seattle-based coffee giant is facing increasingly tough competition, and sales are slowing down. Recently, Starbucks brought in a new CEO (Brian Niccol from Chipotle (CMG)) to help kickstart the overall growth of the iconic coffee chain.

While Niccol has a lot of experience turning around major food chains, the biggest issue Starbucks faces at the moment isn’t even with its business model. Labor risk is becoming a huge problem for the company as more stores unionize.

Starbucks stores have been undergoing a mass wave of unionization during the last two years, with over 500 stores unionized already. The biggest impact of this is that the National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) has become critical of Starbucks and how they operate their stores (more on this later). This is an enormous risk to their business because Starbucks is not in a high-margin business.

Unions are beginning to catch on to the automation trend that is spreading across food prep and manufacturing. To combat this, they’re also demanding in contract negotiations that their workforces and jobs not be automated. Starbucks depends on future automation growth to help fuel reinvestment back into the business. Missing this automation trend is a huge growth drag

To be clear, I think Brian Niccol is a great leader. But all leaders are imperfect and have their faults. For Brian, I don’t see how one of his strengths is unions (Chipotle stores are not unionized anywhere near the same level). Unions bring a unique challenge for Brian, and I don’t think the stock is pricing this in. With this, I think Starbucks shares are a sell. On a P/E basis, they’re expensive, and I think any upside will likely be capped by labor risk.

Background

Starbucks was founded in 1971 by Gordon Bowker, Zev Siegl and Jerry Baldwin. The three men and their vision for a different type of coffee shop was launched from their first location in Seattle, Washington. Throughout the ‘70s, Starbucks performed well, but accelerated in the 1980s.

In 1982, Howard Schultz got connected with the coffee brand and developed a passion for not only coffee, but the inviting, romantic atmosphere that allowed for people to connect and converse with each other over a cup of coffee. Schultz served as Starbucks’ CEO for a total of 23 years.

Over the last three years, as companies across the globe have worked to rebound from the effects of the COVID-19 pandemic, Starbucks has had an increasingly difficult time. Newer and smaller niche coffee shops have sprouted up, resulting in Starbucks losing out on its target audience and losing its spot in many cities as the preferred location for people to show up, connect and converse.

Niccol, though, is supposed to be the answer Starbucks bulls are looking for. The newly named CEO has played a critical role in the turnarounds of Chipotle and Taco Bell, and he has the opportunity to do the same thing with Starbucks.

Niccol spent seven years at Taco Bell, and he joined the company during a time in which sales had stalled significantly. Following his seven-year stint with Taco Bell, Niccol became the CEO of Chipotle, where he helped double revenue, increase profits by almost seven times and increase the stock price of the company by nearly eight times.

The big difference between these turnaround stories and what Niccol is facing today is labor risk. And I think this is a big one.

According to a Reuters article from June, Starbucks had roughly 400 locations throughout the United States that had unionized, which involved more than 10,000 employees. The unionization efforts are clearly picking up steam, though, with the number of unionized locations now topping 500 stores.

Why Unions Are A Unique Problem For Brian Niccol

Unionization is a unique problem for Niccol, and it’s something that he has not had to deal with in the past. As of August, Chipotle had only one unionized store in the entire United States.

Taco Bell, the other restaurant Niccol was CEO of, is also completely incomparable to Starbucks as it relates to a unionized workforce.

This issue is something Niccol has not had to work through in the past, and it could provide him and Starbucks with a unique roadblock in the future.

During his time at Chipotle, one of the things Niccol pushed for the hardest was increased efficiency gains both in portion sizes and ingredient usage to improve margins. I wrote previously about these concerns with Chipotle in the spring before Niccol left the company.

Now with increasing unionization pressure at Starbucks, Niccol faces a key challenge.

The proportion of Starbucks locations that are unionized is still very small, but it's growing fast. Of the reported 16,482 locations in the United States, only just over 500 are unionized. That is just over 3% of locations that are unionized. But this means the majority of the labor risk from these unionizations is ahead of the coffee chain, not behind it. The probability that workers at these locations push back on the efficiency gains that Niccol will likely go for is fairly high.

Valuation

As the coffee chain’s CEO was replaced, Starbucks has seen earnings estimates largely move downward over the last three months as Wall Street analysts begin to price in the fact that a turnaround will likely be painful.

I think with this, the market has also started to price in rising labor costs, which will be a big deal for margins.

Currently, Starbucks sports a gross profit margin of 27.61%, which is higher than their 5-year average of 26.32%. Their net income margin of 11.16% is also higher than their 5-year average of 10.39% and significantly higher than the sector median of 4.64%.

In essence, they are likely over earning right now. My fear is that unions driving wages higher will make these metrics revert to the mean. This means lower EPS if revenue does not pick up. And with revenue estimates moving downward, we may not see the lift that’s needed.

Shares currently trade in the mid-20s forward P/E ratio. The forward non-GAAP P/E ratio of 26.72 is significantly higher than the sector median number of 18.58. This doesn’t make sense to me, as I think margins face a real risk. I think the forward P/E should probably be closer to 20 to reflect the labor risks the coffee giant faces. If the P/E moved closer to 20, which would represent 25.14% downside.

Bull Thesis

In my opinion, the biggest bull thesis for Starbucks is that the NLRB was struck down in June by the US Supreme Court for overstepping some of their rulings. The NLRB was trying to take action against Starbucks for firing workers as they sought to unionize. The upside from this ruling is that bulls could argue this is a sign that the unionization drive reached a dead end.

From the June court ruling, the Supreme Court sided with Starbucks, ruling that Starbucks does not have to reinstate its workers while the administrative case continues.

The justices unanimously threw out a lower court's approval of an injunction sought by the U.S. National Labor Relations Board (NLRB) ordering Starbucks to reinstate the workers while the agency's in-house administrative case against the Seattle-based company proceeds.

The Reuters article also revealed that the NLRB has received hundreds of complaints accusing Starbucks of “unlawful labor practices such as firing union supporters, spying on workers and closing stores during labor campaigns.”

Starbucks, though, has denied all of these claims. It’s this second line about hundreds of complaints that I find concerning. Because while Starbucks may or may not end up being found guilty for firing workers who attempted to unionize, the coffee chain would be in much bigger trouble if there has been a larger, orchestrated attempt underway to limit unionization. This is illegal in the United States.

I do not think this Supreme Court ruling will be enough to stop the unionization. Most unionization activities by Starbucks employees are fairly lawful and legitimate. In fact, just earlier this month, the Starbucks Worker's Union (SBWU) signed their 500th store to unionize. I don’t think this trend is going to stop anytime soon. With this, I think this continues to be a real risk to their business model.

Takeaway

The wave of unionization that is impacting Starbucks is unlike frankly anything we have seen at a fast food chain in the US. It's also a largely new and unique experience for their new CEO, Brian Niccol. Niccol has great experience turning around other fast food companies (Chipotle and Taco Bell), but neither of those two had a strong push to unionize like Starbucks currently is facing.

Unionization will continue to be a major risk to Starbucks business model and will not go away. I think this will push margins down in the near future. Shareholders will likely suffer.

At this point, I just don’t see what the growth drivers are to justify why the stock trades at such a high forward price-to-earnings ratio. On the surface, this higher price to earnings ratio could be justified by the new CEO. But Niccol faces these unique challenges. I believe his management technique is incongruent with what the labor force is set to do at Starbucks.

With this, I am a sell on shares of the coffee giant. If we saw a change in unionization trends at Starbucks, then I may be more likely to change my outlook.

data.title: Discovery Silver: Very Promising Project, 2 Points That Hurt Me, Though
data.description:
Discovery Silver (OTCQX:DSVSF) has been on my watchlist for quite a while now. I am searching for promising silver companies which check many boxes to be really of interest for me:

  • low production costs
  • a high share of silver of total production
  • long reserve life (true reserves, not just lower-category resources!)
  • either in production or at least close to production
  • a proven management team
  • a not too high valuation

While Discovery Silver is still a couple of years away from its potential start of production, its presented economics look very promising. That's why I want to discuss this case, which belongs on a good watchlist.

Unheard of economics

The company is developing the world's biggest known silver mining deposit in Mexico, the Cordero mining project. In many aspects, the economics of this project are almost incredibly superb in this otherwise messy sector.

In its investor presentation, Discovery shows its key figures.

Just to pick a few points: Discovery Silver's Cordero mine has an expected mine life of 19 years (potentially more to come). This is twice as high as e.g. is the case with high-quality miner SilverCrest Metals (SILV). Further, Discovery's production costs on an all-in sustaining basis ('AISC') are way below the industry's average, which is estimated to be in the mid-20s USD. The planned yearly production - once online - would be not less than the world's biggest from a single mine.

We are talking about a giant in the making, not just another silver mine like many others.

On the chart above, you can see that in a single year, the Cordero mine is planned to dig out almost 35 million ounces. To put this into perspective, there are currently just two other mines which achieve more than 15 million ounces on a yearly basis!

The management holds ~5% of the stock of Discovery Silver, which I like to see. Speaking of management, what I also like is that the company is led by Tony Makuch - the CEO who formed Kirkland Lake Gold between 2016-2022 into a major success story.

Kirkland Lake Gold was a Canadian pure play gold producer with ultra-low-cost operations in Canada and Australia. Its stock was on fire and even trading at multiples in the high 20s because it generated tons of free cash flow while the competition was asleep. The company, however, a few years ago was sold to the world's number three, Agnico Eagle Mines (AEM) (see here), and Makuch soon left. Unfortunately, Kirkland is not a standalone stock anymore.

The happier I was to see Makuch being the new CEO of Discovery Silver.

The risk factors to watch

Looking at the valuation, Discovery looks to be more on the cheap side - a bit strange at first sight for such a gigantic project with best in class economics and an expected long mine life of almost 20.

The company in its latest feasibility study (the last big study before construction can start) from February 2024 shows a net present value of USD1.8 billion - while it is trading only for a market cap of USD260 million (CAD~360 million). One could argue that they are using market prices for the metals and not more conservative figures with a discount to spot prices.

However, at 0.15x its NPV it's given away almost for free - it seems.

For comparison, I like to make a comparison to the soon-to-be acquired SilverCrest Metals because it is another high-quality silver miner with among the lowest production costs.

SilverCrest Metals was not even trading at such a low multiple when it released its pre economic assessment in 2019 (PEA, with more studies to follow to confirm the project's economics, i.e. more uncertainty, see here).

SilverCrest's NPV was an estimated USD400 million, while the company had a market cap of CAD~250 million or USD~180 million - give or take 0.5x its NPV or three times the current valuation of Discovery Silver - at a much earlier stage of the development process and much lower silver prices.

From then on, SilverCrest only became more and more expensive, despite still being years away from construction, not to mention production. It was also clear that financing would be needed - as management didn't take on debt in order to preserve a strong balance sheet, dilution through equity raises was the only option.

This is the thing with Discovery Silver - despite its impressive numbers, the project is not financed yet. From my research, I have the number of USD600 million in needed capital before me. That's quite a lot in comparison to the market cap, which is not even half of that figure. This means the dilutive effect would be significant. Cash on the balance sheet per the latest half-year results was less than USD30 million, so no support from that side.

And by the way, costs have risen by a third since the last estimate.

Discovery Silver currently is about two years away from production, as management showed in their presentation in February.

With financing still unclear, a discount is absolutely justified because it is a clear risk factor. Current equity holders likely will get diluted, even if debt were a component of the financing package.

What's likely also and maybe even primarily holding the stock down is the fact that Cordero is an open-pit mine. That's the type of mine the new Mexican government aims to forbid.

After the above news came out, please have a look how the stock of Discovery Silver reacted:

The stock is back to where it fell from as this news broke out by now. Nonetheless, I remain skeptical and not fully convinced to buy shares at this stage. In its latest MD&A (management discussion and analysis) for H1 2024, management explicitly added this risk to their risk section.

Recently, there has been some good news, too, respectively not so bad ones. The authors in the linked article write that the new Mexican president does not seem to be in a rush to enforce a ban on open-pit mining.

Risks to my thesis

The silver price is high and a good chunk of above-average production costs. Many miners have seen their stocks rise too so that they could use their stock for acquisitions. The appetite for deals has already started to increase with the takeover of SilverCrest Metals. Discovery Silver is clearly a lucrative target.

The stock could also react positively in case a final financing package is announced, even if it contained much dilution because the insecurity would be gone.

Conclusion

All in all, a mixed bag. On one hand, a very compelling setup, when looking at the mining project and its economics.

On the other two big challenges. The first, financing, would likely be somehow solvable. My strategy would have been to wait until financing is secured and announced to better assess the potential effect of a likely dilution to shareholders. An entry at a much de-risked stage could result in some missed performance, but the dilution would be avoided.

The second risk, from the political side, is what made me finally go to the sidelines. I cannot seriously assess that.

I will continue to monitor this case because it is too interesting not to do so. But there's absolutely no need to rush in.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Week 189: $18,900 Allocated, $1,775.79 In Projected Dividends
data.description:
The markets had a good week as Inflation and the U.S. Producer Price Inflation slowed. The latest CPI report was the 6th consecutive monthly decline for inflation as it reached 2.4%. U.S. factory gate prices were flat in September rather than increasing by 0.2% as they did in August. The S&P 500 is at record highs, closing at 5,815.03, and it climbed 1.42% this week. The S&P has increased 21.91% YTD as it started the year just below 4,800. Many firms have continued to raise their price targets, and earlier in the year, the idea of 5,600 or 5,800 seems unrealistic, but now, reaching 6,000 by the end of the year seems entirely possible. The Nasdaq also had a good week as it increased by 1.45% to 18,342.94. All the economic indicators seem to be falling into place for a continued bull market. The last hurdle we need to get past is the election, and the only reason why I don't think we're seeing as much volatility as some expected is because it looks like we will have gridlock in Washington through a split government. Unless an unforeseen macroeconomic event occurs, I think that the economy is setting up for a lucrative business environment that can take the markets higher. I will continue to invest each week and look forward to building out the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

Another week and another all-time high. This week was a bit different as the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio reached an all-time high in account value and profitability, but not in return on investment. After allocating $18,900 to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio, the account balance finished week 189 at $23,551. The account gained $7.99 in profitability and is now up $4,651 on invested capital or 24.61%. This week, I added to my positions in Ford (F) and Blue Owl Capital Corporation (OBDC). The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio generated $39.12 in dividend income this week. The combination of reinvesting the generated income and making these investments increased my forward projected annualized dividend income by $11.58 (0.66%) to $1,775.79. I think that by the end of October, it's entirely possible that I will be generating over $1,800 in forward dividend income. It's turning out to be a good year, and from an income perspective, I think that I can finish the year generating over $1,900 in forward dividend income heading into 2025.

The Overall Performance of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio

I started this account with $100 and have added $100 per week since inception. My primary investment goals are to generate recurring dividend income while mitigating downside risk. My secondary goal is to generate capital appreciation, as this is an income-focused portfolio. I don't allow any positions to exceed 5% of the portfolio, and try not to let any sectors exceed 20%. At some point, the market will turn, and a selloff will occur. As we have seen in the past, the vast diversification throughout the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio has allowed me to get through some difficult economic environments without much deterioration. I am very happy with the results as the portfolio is generating $1,775.79 in forward dividend income, which is a portfolio yield of 7.54% and a yield on cost of 9.4%. My investment methodology is living up to my investment goals, and I am excited to see how the remainder of the year progresses.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Dividend Section

Here's how much dividend income is generated per investment basket:

  • Equities $486.57 (27.4%)
  • ETFs $469.29 (26.43%)
  • CEFs $306.09 (17.24%)
  • REITs $303.76 (17.11%)
  • BDCs $210.07 (11.83%)

Collecting dividends can serve many functions in a portfolio. Some investors utilize dividends to supplement their income and live off of them. I'm building a dividend portfolio for myself 30 years into the future. In 2022, I collected $507.80 in dividend income from 533 dividends. In 2023, I collected $978.11 in dividend income from 660 dividends. After 40 weeks in 2024, I have collected $1,204.64 from 559 dividends. This is 123.41% of the total dividend income generated in 2023 from 84.7% of the dividends produced.

After generating $39.12 in dividend income this week, my weekly average income increased to $29.38. This is an increase of $12.47 or 73.69% YoY. 2024 is winding down, and I am generating significantly more income than in 2023. By the end of the year, I think I can generate between $30 and $33 in average weekly income. Last year, the largest amount of weekly income I generated was $41.13, and I have more than doubled that twice this year. There is a chance that I can generate my first week of $100 dividend income in 2024, and I think I will generate $100 in several weeks during 2025.

This is certainly a change from last month. We still have 2 ½ weeks left in October, and I have already generated $77.80 in dividend income this month. Last year, October was the second-largest month of dividend income generated, right behind November. I think Q4 is going to be a big quarter, and I may be able to set a new monthly record above $160. When I look at the YoY growth that continues to occur, it makes me excited about the future and to continue allocating capital to the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio.

There are still 34 positions generating at least 1 share per year from reinvesting their dividends. The additional shares are expected to generate $122.47 in additional income. When I look at the positions that are generating between 50-99% of their share price in dividends, I think that I can get at least 5 of them to cross over into the green section of the table below by the end of the year, and I might be able to get closer to 10 positions. I have been working on Ford, and there are a lot of positions I want to add more capital to as the months go by. Eventually, I would like to have half of the portfolio generating at least 1 share per year from their dividends.

The Dividend Harvesting Portfolio Composition

The amount of the portfolio that REITs represent continues to decline, and pretty soon I would like for both REITs and ETFs to be under 19%. In the coming weeks, I think that ETFs will become the largest sector as there are several I want to add to because they are on the verge of generating at least 1 share from their dividends. I am expecting an uptick in ETFs before they head lower, but I do think REITs will be under 19% before Thanksgiving. I am trying to focus on more individual equities, and one of my goals for 2025 will be to work on evening the portfolio out a bit further.

Individual equities now represent 38.76% of the Dividend Harvesting portfolio while generating 27.40% of the dividend income. REITs, ETFs, CEFs, and BDCs make up 61.24% of the portfolio and generate 72.60% of the forward income. I am working on getting individual equities to represent more of the portfolio. The long-term goal is to have the sectors be close to equal weight, but that may not happen for 5-10 years at this rate, if ever.

Starwood Property Trust (STWD) has been replaced by Simon Property Group (SPG) in the top-10 holdings. STWD spent a long time on the top-10 list. I am happy that the holdings are starting to round out a bit further. Altria Group (MO) appreciated a bit this week after it paid a large dividend and now represents 4.44% of the portfolio. Outside of MO, I am very happy with how my capital is spread out. Over time, I would like MO to get below 4% of the portfolio. I don't intend on any positions exceeding 5% of the portfolio and won't be adding to MO anytime soon.

MO paid a big dividend this week of $21.01, and after being reinvested, it purchased 0.4173 shares. MO has now generated $138.01 in dividend income since the beginning of the series and purchased 3.01 shares from its dividends. The metrics from the table below will be a bit different from last week, as SPG overtook STWD for the 10th spot. My allocation to the top 10 positions is $5,268.28, and they finished the week with a value of $7,065.07. This is an ROI of $1,796.79 (34.11%). There has been $684.38 of dividends generated and reinvested, which is 12.99% of the initial investment into these positions. I am now projecting that $524.95 of dividend income will be generated from the top 10 holdings, which have a forward yield of 9.96%. These positions represent 30% of the portfolio, while their forward dividend income is projected to generate 29.56% of the Dividend Harvesting Portfolio's annualized income.

Week 189 Additions

In week 189 I added to my positions in Ford and Blue Owl Capital Corporation.

Ford

  • I added another share to Ford because I am working on getting it to produce 1 share per year from its dividends
  • I believe that the lower rate environment will be good for Ford as purchasing or leasing vehicles will be more affordable as rates come down
  • Ford is still generating a dividend yield that exceeds 7%, and I want to lock in the yield on cost while I still can

Blue Owl Capital Corporation

  • I recently wrote an article about OBDC outlining my investment thesis (can be read here)
  • OBDC is one of my favorite BDCs as it generates the 3rd largest amount of net investment income (NII) in its peer group
  • I think OBDC represents great value as it trades at 7.61 times its NII, and at a -3.32% discount to its NAV while generating a double-digit yield when the supplemental dividends are accounted for
  • I think the lower rate environment will be good for BDCs as they will be able to generate more loan originations and lower their cost of borrowing

Week 190 Game Plan

Next week, I will be adding a position from the read suggestions, so please leave all of your ideas in the comment section below.

Conclusion

The dividend income is flowing and compounding as the chart below continues to climb up and to the right. My investment objectives of generating recurring income while mitigating downside risk are being accomplished, and I couldn't be happier. The amount of forward dividend income produced continues to grow rapidly, and my weekly projected dividend income average is up to $34.15. I plan on continuing to purchase income-producing assets in this account for a long time as it will become a tool to fund my retirement in the future. Next week, I will be selecting a position from the reader's suggestions to invest in, so please leave all of your ideas in the comment section. I look forward to interacting with everyone.

data.title: Copa Holdings, S.A.: A Solid Value Opportunity With A Superior Yield
data.description:
Copa Holdings, S.A. (NYSE:CPA) is an airline company operating from its central hub in Panama City. The company boasts an expansive flight network throughout the Americas, and, with the success of its low-cost business model, has been seeing its number of passengers climb by double digits. Its operational success also includes driving down its cost per average seat miles, highlighting strong execution on the cost control side. In addition to a very attractive dividend yield of 6.6%, the stock also boasts the highest EBITDA growth among its peers at the lowest multiples. I rate the stock as a buy.

Overview

Copa Holdings, S.A. is a Panama-based holding company that owns Copa Airlines, a leading Latin American passenger airline and cargo carrier. The company has a market cap exceeding $4 billion, and with a low-cost business model, is among the leading players in the region.

As of the year-end in 2023, the company operates a fleet of 106 aircraft of various Boeing models. With its Panama base of operations acting as the hub to its expansive network, Copa Airlines connects passengers to and from various locations in South, Central, and North America, as well as the Caribbean islands.

In its central positioning in Panama, which lies in the crossroads of the Americas, Copa Air has the strategic advantage of an extremely valuable location. This gives it access to some of the most high economic growth and densely populated regions in the Western hemisphere. As a result, the company is well-positioned in the industry to capitalize on-demand strength and sustained growth. It has been active in increasing its fleet size to enhance passenger capacity, whilst simultaneously working on expanding its network to include more destinations.

As we shall see below, the company continues to make solid progress on the operational front, with a strong focus on cost reduction, in order to enhance overall profitability.

Q2 Assessment

Operationally speaking, Q2 was among the best quarters the company experienced, especially in terms of cost efficiency, which makes the company highly attractive compared to its peers in the airlines industry.

Despite Q2 being among the seasonally weakest quarters for the company, the company saw its passenger traffic climb by 10.6%, compared to Q2 in 2023, indicating booming demand for its services. Compared to Q2 last year, which saw 2.98 million passengers, this time the company served 3.30 million passengers.

Not only that, but it also saw its capacity increase by 9.7%, to $7.4 billion in available seat miles. With the increase in both passenger traffic and capacity, Copa managed to drive up its load factor by 0.7%, showing efficient management of capacity based on climbing demand.

While all of the metrics mentioned above broadly paint a promising picture of progress, I am especially drawn to the strong execution on the cost side. In terms of unit costs excluding fuel, or the Cost per Average Seat Mile (CASM), Copa saw a 5.8% YoY decline owing to reduced sales and distribution costs, as well as lower aircraft maintenance costs.

The improvement on the sales and distribution side comes as more customers are booking flights directly through the airlines, and by-passing third-party channels like travel agents. There has also been more utilization of the NDC travel agency channel, which typically acts as a lower-cost platform. Moreover, on the maintenance side, the company enjoyed a positive adjustment in leased aircraft return provisions. Together, each of these factors have led to a very attractive cost structure in the current quarter.

Overall, although the company saw its EBITDA drop by 11.3%, during the quarter to $239 million, its operational metrics are all very positive. Moreover, the company's operating margin of 19.47% is the second highest Q2 margin recorded in the company's history.

I must also point out that the company is keen on returning value to its shareholders by means of dividend payments. The company follows a consistent policy of paying out 40% of the prior year's adjusted earnings to shareholders. Currently, CPA boasts a very attractive dividend yield (ttm) of 6.6%. This is the highest yield on any airline stock, by a significant margin:

CPA was impacted by the Covid hit the entire aviation industry took, and as a result, stopped dividend payments between 2021 and 2023. Now that the industry is back on its feet, and the company is generating operational cash flows of nearly over $1 billion annually, it has reinstated its dividend policy, rewarding its shareholders with its stellar gains.

Valuation

I strongly believe that CPA is undervalued, and I have the strong arguments for this case. Firstly, compared to its peers, the company boasts the highest forward-looking EBITDA growth rate, at the lowest non-GAAP PE (FY1) ratio.

To demonstrate this, I have selected five additional peers for the company from the passenger airline industry, each of which have a market cap ranging from $1 billion to $8 billion. These companies are shown below:

For each of the companies, I have decided to plot the EBITDA growth rate (FWD) against the FY1 Non-GAAP PE ratios on a scatter chart. Note that all the data I have used has been collected from the growth and valuation pages of each respective company from Seeking Alpha. The relationship between these metrics is shown below:

I have added a trend line to see the general relationship between future growth and PE ratio. Based on this, it is pretty clear that CPA offers the highest growth, at the lowest price, making it the most valuable play in the peer group.

The second reason, is the company's forward EV/EBITDA ratio of 4.52. Not only is this among the lowest in the company's peer group, it is also significantly below CPA's own historical average of 20.03. This means the company's forward EV/EBITDA is presently 77.42% lower than the average it has been known to trade at in the last 5 years. To me, this seriously spells an undervaluation, which is why I rate the stock as a buy.

Risk

While I find CPA's profile to be extremely promising, with its operational strides and its value proposition, of course, my thesis isn't without risk. However, as opposed to one major risk, there is a range of minor risks, which, if were to all play out, could impact the company's operational performance. Of these, I will mention the two that I find most concerning.

Firstly, due to the political volatility in Venezuela, there has been on and off travel disruption to and from the country, which has directly been impacting Copa Airlines' operations there. This is particularly impactful because, as a network carrier, this does not only obstruct the Panama to Venezuela flight routes, but also wider connecting networks. So while flights to Venezuela reflect 2% of CPA's total capacity, the revenue impact is more than double due to connecting flights. Due to these disruptions, the company already slightly trimmed its guidance on unit revenues, for the full year 2024. However, despite already accounting for the situation, further escalation in the country could bode negatively for CPA, and may lead to an even more prolonged period of flight restrictions and reduced demand of flights to and from the region.

Additionally, weakening currencies in the Central and South American regions, particularly in Brazil, which represents a major market for the company, adds a further layer of uncertainty to deal with. This devaluation creates pressure on point-of-sale demand, where local ticket sales in Brazil may weaken as fares become relatively more expensive in the local currency. In such a case, customers may opt for low-cost carriers, such as the Azul Brazilian Airlines.

Despite these risks being somewhat concerning, I am reassured by the fact that they have been acknowledged by the company's management, and factored into the guidance for the year. As a result, I don't see the company being hit by an adverse surprise situation.

Takeaway

Copa Airlines is a well-positioned business with a strategic location that allows it to capitalize on strong demand trends coming from the high population and high economic growth regions in the Americas. The company has been executing well on its low-cost model, bringing down its cost per average seat miles significantly, all while managing surging capacity and a jump in passenger numbers. Its dividend yield of 6.6% is the highest in the airlines industry, and its EBITDA growth is the most attractive. With all these strengths, the stock still has the lowest non-GAAP PE ratio (FY1). I think CPA is very valuable, and therefore, I rate the stock as a buy.

data.title: Microsoft: It's Time To Look Elsewhere (Rating Downgrade)
data.description:
When I last covered Microsoft (NASDAQ:MSFT) with a “buy” rating on April 29th 2024 in “Microsoft: Vulnerable to Downside Corrections”, the stock was in the process of falling from the April 11th highs of $429.37 (in a move that resulted in losses of -9.26%). Overall, the thesis of my bullish article was that these were the types of declines that tend to produce in the best buying opportunities for this stock and that those bearish moves represented a new opportunity to add to long positions:

Following my publication, MSFT share prices quickly reversed higher and generated gains of 16.71% over the next nine weeks, showing that well-structured dip buying strategies can be an excellent approach to maximizing gains:

Since then, Microsoft has released quarterly results for the fiscal fourth-quarter period that surpassed consensus estimates for both earnings and revenue. However, weaknesses within the Azure cloud segment were enough to worry investors, and the stock quickly gave back all of its prior gains while falling to near-term lows of $385.60 on August 5th. Specifically, EPS figures came in at $2.95 (slightly beating estimates calling for $2.93 per share) and revenues came in at $64.73 billion (surpassing estimates calling for $64.39 billion). Additionally, the quarter’s net income figure of $22 billion indicates annualized growth rates of 10% while gross margin figures posted annualized gains of 14%:

Overall, revenue growth came in at 15% and Microsoft’s guidance figures suggest current quarter revenues are likely to post within a $63.8-64.8 billion range. At the midpoint, this would imply revenue growth of just under 14% for the period. However, given the market’s most recent negative reaction to earnings, it seems these results (if realized) might not be enough to generate significant share price rallies near-term.

In my view, part of the reason for this lies in the fact that Microsoft’s forward price-earnings valuation (at 31.88x) suggests that there might be better options right now within the MAG-7 complex. To be fair, Tesla, Inc. (TSLA) is currently trading with an extremely high forward price-earnings valuation of 95.17x while Nvidia Corp. (NVDA) is also relatively elevated levels of 48.56x. Apple, Inc. (AAPL) is a bit more in-line with Microsoft’s current valuation (at 31.26x). Two stock choices that are looking much more well-positioned at the moment can be found with Meta Platforms (META) at 27.61x and Alphabet, Inc. (GOOG) at just 21.75x.

An even worse picture starts to emerge if we look at these same companies through the lens of the forward price-sales metric, where only Nvidia (at 26.95x) is trading with a higher ratio relative to Microsoft (at 11.16x). Of course, this means that five of these seven stocks are currently trading at lower valuations - and some of these selections are significantly cheaper. META (at 9.23x) and AAPL (at 8.38x) are somewhat close using this specific valuation metric. However, TSLA (at 6.99x), GOOG (at 5.89x) and AMZN (at just 3.1x) are looking much more attractive right now.

Last, I’d like to look at the relative price-book ratio that is seen throughout this mega-cap cohort. Once again, Microsoft is trading within the upper half of this group (at 11.6x) while Nvidia (at 58.19x) and Apple (at 52.74x) are obviously much more expensive. However, once again, we are seeing several MAG-7 options that are trading at much lower valuations at the moment. Specifically, we can point to Tesla (at 10.53x), Amazon (at 8.33x), Meta Platforms (at 9.52x) and Alphabet (at just 6.81x) as potentially preferable options. When we are looking at these comparative valuation ratios, one attractive MAG-7 stock that stands out to me in particular is Alphabet - and this is why I recently upgraded my GOOG rating to a “strong buy” in my article “Google: Buy Before Earnings” on October 14th.

Given the general weakness that we have seen in Microsoft’s most recent post-earnings share price performances, I am a bit reluctant to recommend adding to long positions at current levels. Additionally, we are starting to see the formation of what might actually be a head and shoulders pattern on the daily time frames. If this type of price pattern became apparent on a shorter-term time frame, the overall significant of the formation might be reduced dramatically. However, the daily chart is arguably the most commonly watched time frame in the entire market, so it stands to reason that many other investors might be seeing this bearish pattern as well. Thus, if we combine this price activity with recent market tendencies to sell shares of MSFT following earnings and the underwhelming forward valuation metrics that characterize Microsoft in relation to its MAG-7 peer group, it becomes easier to see why I am currently downgrading my outlook for this stock to a “hold” rating.

In order for me to change this viewpoint, I would need to see markets respond favorably to Microsoft’s next earnings release and engage in enough buying activity to send share prices through important resistance levels that are now found at the September 19th, 2024 highs (of $441.50). If this occurs, my next upside price target for this stock would rest at the stock's all-time highs of $468.35 (which was recorded during the July 5th, 2024 trading session). Unfortunately, guidance figures for the fiscal first quarter look a bit unimpressive at the moment, with the possible exception of the company’s expected figures for the Azure Cloud segment (with annualized growth estimates currently found between 28-29% for the period). However, growth rates for this segment were previously seen at similar levels and this failed to elicit a bullish response from the market (and the stock ultimately declined in the periods that followed).

For these reasons, I think that we will need to see upside surprises within this segment that are above 30% in order to see MSFT share prices break the aforementioned resistance levels. Conversely, another post-earnings selloff would actually cause me to further reduce my rating outlook for this stock (to a “sell” rating) in the event that share prices breach key support zones currently found at $385.58 (which is the historical low from August 5th, 2024). In this case, we would be seeing a downside break in the “neckline” of the head and shoulders pattern that I outlined previously, and this is the type of event that could cause share price momentum to accelerate in the bearish direction. Of course, this would be the most bearish extent of my current expectations for this stock, but this is the type of outcome that I would need to see before considering selling my MSFT position. Overall, I am a bit disheartened by the fact that other stocks within the MAG-7 cohort are trading with more attractive forward valuations right now, and this is the sentiment that really forms the basis for my decision to downgrade Microsoft to my current “hold” rating.

data.title: Wesdome Gold Mines: Turnaround Thesis Remains Intact
data.description:
All figures are in United States Dollars unless otherwise noted with a C$ in front of the figure. G/T = grams per tonne (of gold). GEOs = gold-equivalent ounces. AISC refers to all-in sustaining costs. LOMP = life of mine plan. TPD = tonnes per day.

Q2 Production & Sales

Wesdome Gold Mines Ltd. (“Wesdome”)(TSX:WDO:CA) (OTCQX:WDOFF) released its Q2-24 results in mid-August, reporting record quarterly production of ~44,000 ounces of gold, representing a 42% increase in the year-ago period. The sharp increase in production can be attributed to the successful ramp of up Kiena and mining of high-grade Kiena Deep ore that began in mid-April, providing a significant boost to Kiena’s quarterly performance. In fact, production at its new Kiena Mine more than tripled on a year-over-year basis to ~24,800 ounces, edging out its legacy Eagle River Mine. Not surprisingly, this leverage on fixed costs with much higher production and sales translated to far better cost performance, with Kiena’s all-in sustaining costs [AISC] improving from $2,052/oz to $1,123/oz.

Digging into the Q2-24 results a little closer, Kiena processed ~57,700 tonne at 13.5 G/T of gold, a ~170% increase from 5.0 G/T of gold in the year-ago period. As noted, the higher grades benefited from a much higher proportion of ultra high-grade Kiena Deep ore vs. a greater reliance on the lower grade Martin, S50 and VC zones as the company worked to access the 129 Level. The good news is that Kiena is now benefiting from these significantly higher grades, and the company noted that mined grades at Kiena exceeded its expectations with a hockey-stick like increase in grades vs. the gradual ramp up previously anticipated. In fact, Wesdome’s former CEO stated that “grades are comparing favorably to our short-term model” and that productivity was in line with expectations.

Just as importantly as mined grades, recoveries improved further year-over-year with the benefit of high-grade ore, with Kiena’s recovery rates coming in at 99%, confirming the elevated recoveries expected in the 2021 PFS (98.5%) from the Kiena Deep zones. It’s also worth noting that the significantly improved AISC (Q2-24: $1,123/oz) would have been even better and closer to ~$1,050/oz if not for fewer ounces sold than produced. Finally, Wesdome looks positioned to deliver on Kiena guidance of 85,000 ounces, with ~33,200 ounces produced year-to-date but a back-end-weighed H2-24 with the benefit of full access to Kiena Deep ore vs. just three months of mining in H1-24.

The latter would be a welcome development, as would be delivering on its annual guidance midpoint of 170,000 ounces. This is because Wesdome is currently sporting the worst three-year trailing track record of delivery on guidance vs. its midpoint, with an average 23.5% miss vs. guidance. In fairness, Wesdome's new CEO seems to be doing a better job managing expectations since joining Wesdome at the beginning of Q3-23, with Wesdome tracking well to beat its 2024 guidance (~45.5% based on year-to-date production of ~77,400 ounces) with 55%+ expected in H2-24.

Moving over to the company's Eagle River Mine, production came in at ~19,300 ounces, a 15% decline from the year-ago period. Lower production was related to lower throughput (~52,600 tonnes) and lower grades, offset by slightly higher grades (11.8 G/T of gold) and marginally lower recoveries in the period. Wesdome noted that the dip in throughput was partially related to a maintenance shut in the last week of Q2. Unfortunately, the combination of fewer ounces sold, and higher sustaining capital resulted in a spike in AISC to $1,860/oz, up 26% year-over-year, and costs will remain well above the industry average in H2-24. Wesdome stated the following in relation to the asset:

"Optimization remains a key priority at Eagle River, where costs have escalated over the last number of years. We have identified initiatives that are at various stages of execution and anticipate that results will yield value over the next year. We're in the process of examining productivity drivers, cost inputs, and our maintenance practices for value."

- Wesdome Mines Q2-24 Conference Call.

As for these initiatives, Wesdome noted that it should be able to share more by the end of this year, with one being the potential to fill the 1,200 TPD of permitted mill capacity to better leverage fixed costs. On a positive note, while this will be a lower production and higher-cost year at Eagle River (guidance midpoint: 85,000 ounces at $1,625/oz AISC), Eagle River is gearing up for a better 2025 which should result in an improved cost profile if its optimization work is successful. Plus, while the increase in costs is disappointing, it's at least being more than offset by the record gold price which looks like it could average $2,500/oz in H2-24.

To summarize, Q2-24 was a solid quarter overall, with Kiena Deep ore helping to offset a softer quarter for production and sales at Eagle River. Meanwhile, the heavy lifting is done at Kiena and grades are reconciling well to date, a positive sign relative to the weaker ramp-ups we've seen at other high-grade underground mines in Canada over the past few years (Madsen — Ontario, Premier — British Columbia). Let's take a look at costs and margins:

Costs & Margins

Looking at Wesdome's Q2-24 cost performance, AISC came in at $1,445/oz, a material improvement from the $1,666/oz reported in the year-ago period. This was despite higher sustaining capital year-over-year with unit costs benefiting from record production and far lower costs at Kiena which is now performing closer to design with it milling nearly half an ounce per tonne ore. The company also benefited from a slightly weaker Canadian Dollar in Q2-24. However, with two of the largest gold producers in Canada ramping up production near its operations (Magino, Island Gold, Kirkland Lake Camp, Canadian Malartic), it's possible Wesdome and other smaller Canadian miners could see some pressure on labor going forward.

Looking at Wesdome's margins, it saw a material increase in margins to $888/oz vs. $300/oz in the year-ago period. This translated to a 2200+ basis point increase in AISC margins to 38.1%, benefiting from lapping easy year-over-year comps given that Kiena was still processing lower grades as it developed towards the 129 Level. Wesdome also benefited from a record average realized gold price of $2,333/oz. We will see further margin expansion with a full quarter of production from Kiena Deep and what should be an average realized gold price of $2,450/oz or better in Q3-24. Notably, the Q3-24 margin outlook will be helped by lapping a weak quarter for gold in Q3-23, with AISC margins set to soar as Wesdome laps another quarter of easy comps (Q3-23 AISC margins: 13.3%).

Given the significant increase in production and margins in Q2-24, Wesdome's financial results also improved materially. In fact, revenue soared 51% to C$127.8 million, operating cash flow more than tripled to ~C$57 million, and the company was able to fully repay its revolving credit facility, ending the quarter with over C$50 million in cash. Meanwhile, free cash flow increased to ~C$28 million despite higher capex, and Wesdome is on track to generate well over C$100 million in free cash flow this year.

This is a significant improvement from 2023 levels with Wesdome sporting higher production, a more respectable profile and a clean balance sheet to support its planned organic growth. And while the share price has more than reflected this after doubling off its lows, the recent operational execution has improved the investment thesis vs. 18 months ago. Then, there were worries about additional share issuance on the ATM after a slower than planned ramp-up at Kiena.

Recent Developments

As for recent developments, Wesdome noted that it is drilling to test zones that could be mined with its ongoing Presqu'ile ramp development. Presqu'ile could provide a 250+ TPD lift to throughput by late 2025 to take advantage of significant excess capacity at the Kiena Mill, potentially boosting Kiena production by 20,000+ ounces on an annualized basis. Wesdome shared that the Presqu'ile ramp was sitting near 450 meters of a planned 2.2 kilometers, while the 33 Level drift allows for more effective exploration east of Kiena.

It's important to note even with additional ore being sent from Presqu'ile in 2026 that its new Kiena Mine will be operating at less than half its permitted capacity currently of ~2,000 TPD. Hence, there is room to grow this operation even further longer-term and above its expected average annual production profile of ~105,000 ounces with the addition of the Presqu'ile Zone.

As for its Eagle River Mine, Wesdome shared that it made a new discovery closer to the surface with the 6 Central Zone. It was expanded down-plunge to the east and recently reported highlight intercepts of 2.8 meters at 71.7 G/T of gold and 2.6 meters at 93.7 G/T of gold. This zone is near existing infrastructure and has been delineated over a 180-meter plunge and ~150 meters along strike to date. That said, while the highlight intercepts from this new zone are very impressive, we can see that they are quite significant outliers to date. In fact, the average intercept into this zone comes in closer to 1.9 meters at 6.3 G/T of gold when removing the best hole and placing caps on the highest-grade hits. This is ~1.9 meters at 8.5 G/T of gold on average across all intercepts.

Obviously, these average grades are nothing to sneeze at and still quite impressive. There is the potential for high single-digit grades after factoring in mining dilution, but they don't match up to Eagle River's ~400,000 ounce reserve base at 17.4 G/T of gold to date. Elsewhere at Eagle River, Wesdome continues to have success drilling the Falcon 311 one which was extended another 250 meters down-plunge with a highlight intercept of 3.5 meters at 33.0 G/T of gold (31.8 G/T of gold capped).

As for other recent developments, we've seen a significant shake-up in management, which has led to some softness in the stock after significant outperformance in the first half of 2023. This included the unfortunate resignation of COO Frederic-Mercier Langevin which followed the departure of its SVP Exploration & Resources Michael Michaud in July. The former was a significant loss for the company with Frederic-Mercier Langevin having 14 years of experience at Agnico Eagle (AEM) operating multiple mines (Lapa, Goldex, LaRonde) in the region, as well as time spent at one of its largest mines, Meliadine. Wesdome noted that he stepped down for personal reasons, but this is the second COO replacement in less than three years.

As for new appointments to fill these vacant roles, Wesdome recently announced that Guy Belleau would take over the COO role. He was previously spending time as CEO of Sayona Canada (subsidiary of Sayona Mining), as well as COO of ArcelorMittal (MT) and before that, in Mine Manager positions at Horne 5, Eleonore and Perserverance. Meanwhile, Kevin Lonergan has come in as Senior VP Technical Services, with his former role including Director of Mining and VP Mining at Ero Copper (ERO) with Wesdome's new CEO Anthea Bath. Finally, Wesdome has recruited Jono Lawrence who has a strong track record of adding ounces in Africa, including time spent at the massive former Randgold mine, Kibali, in the DRC.

Overall, these appointments have taken some uncertainty off the table and have Wesdome well-equipped on its plan to grow production to 200,000+ ounces after what should be a ~175,000 ounce year in 2024.

Valuation

Based on ~152 million fully diluted shares and a share price of US$9.15, Wesdome continues to trade at a significant premium to its peer group of junior producers (sub 250,000-ounce producers) at a market cap of ~$1.39 billion. In fact, the company currently trades at a valuation of ~$950/oz of total M&I resources and is now back to trading at a premium to its estimated net asset value of ~$1.36 billion. Meanwhile, Wesdome now trades near ~12x FY2025 EV/FCF estimates ($2,300/oz gold price assumption) despite its recent pullback, also a significant premium to some of its larger producer peers.

It's important to note that some premium can easily be justified for its position as a Tier-1 jurisdiction producer with high-grade mines and a declining cost profile, with FY2025 AISC likely to move back below US$1,200/oz. That said, using what I believe to be fair multiples of 8.0x cash flow and 1.2x P/NAV for its premium status as a high-grade Tier-1 producer, I see a fair value for the stock of US$11.20. This points to a 22% upside from current levels which, while attractive to some, is not nearly enough margin of safety for me based on my requirement of a minimum 30% discount to fair value for Tier-1 only jurisdiction junior producers.

Hence, I would need a deeper pullback or low-risk technical setup to become interested in the stock from a swing-trading standpoint.

But what if $2,300/oz gold and $2,400/oz gold in 2025 and 2026 end up being too conservative?

If we take a look at how producers and Wesdome specifically look at $2,500/oz gold and $32.00/oz price assumptions in 2025 and 2026 from a cash flow standpoint, valuations improve materially, with Wesdome's fair value jumping to US$11.80. This still does not provide an adequate margin of safety per my entry rules, and while Wesdome gets more attractive if we assume higher metals prices, so do all other names in the sector. Therefore, higher gold prices do not make Wesdome any more attractive than its peers. In fact, that makes some of its peers in periods of heavier growth capex much more attractive, as it materially improves their free cash flow yields.

To summarize, while there continue to be a handful of extremely attractively names in the sector today that trade at massive discounts to fair value that could easily double or triple if they execute successfully, I don't see enough of a margin of safety in Wesdome today.

Summary

Wesdome Gold Mines Ltd. had another solid quarter in Q2-24 but after a ~100% rally off its lows, I see far more attractive bets elsewhere in the sector. From a bigger picture standpoint, Wesdome should be able to produce ~210,000 ounces at sub $1,150/oz AISC in 2026 and if the gold price holds up, it could easily enjoy a further re-rating to US$12.00 — US$13.00. However, with a weaker pipeline than peers and a far less attractive relative value proposition vs. some of its producer peers, I continue to focus elsewhere in the sector. That said, if WDOFF stock were to pull back closer to US$7.90, I would consider starting a new position in the stock.
